Responses to Conflict: Peace-Making and Peace-Building

PA601-R23-Week 10 – Overview
Section Title Card
 PP Bulb Thumbnail — Week 10 Title Card
PA601-R23-Week 10 – 1.1  ·  Introduction
We'll be discussing a variety of peace and reconciliation interventions. We've talked a lot so far about conflict interventions — mediation, interest-based negotiation, dialogue, building skills and communication, and other techniques. Education, or in the case of a structural issue, building campaigns or movements. All of these are conflict interventions, and many can be used for small-scale or large-scale conflicts.
There is a graph that you can see which simply points out that the traditional interstate armed conflict that we think of as World War II has changed pretty dramatically — armed conflicts now tend to be within states, between government and nongovernmental actors, ethnic groups.
World Battle Deaths Chart
 ...fewer military deaths, as you can see from this graph. Military deaths have gone down dramatically after World War II. However, civilian deaths have gone up dramatically. And this graph looks at the deaths from political violence, things like Rwanda, and notice the dramatic increase in civilian deaths from the kind of wars or violence we now experience globally.
So we are living in a world of conflict — a different kind where societies are being destroyed. Cultures and belief systems. Not the same as traditional interstate conflict. Therefore a whole set of initiatives has been developed to try to help societies return to peace. To discuss the tools used to advance peace, peacemaking, peacekeeping, peace-pushing, peacebuilding, we're bringing back Dr. Bruce Dayton, co-author of the text you've been using.
PA601-R23-Week 10 – 1.2  ·  Interview: Dr. Bruce Dayton
[no voiceover — interview begins]
Interview Title Card
 So I would like to welcome Professor Dayton back to the Maxwell School. I'd like to start with some confusion students sometimes feel navigating the field of conflict transformation and peacemaking...
Interview covers: definitions of peacemaking, peacebuilding, peace-pushing, peacekeeping; Track 1 vs Track 2 diplomacy; the four drivers model of conflict analysis; scale of interventions from grassroots to elite negotiation.
Interaction 1
Respond — Conflict Interventions
Q1: Professor Dayton discussed several categories of conflict interventions. You have been looking into approaches that others have used in addressing conflicts like yours. Where do they fit in the categories described by Professor Dayton?

Feedback: N/A
PA601-R23-Week 10 – 1.3  ·  Peacebuilding and Conflict Transformation
Lederach Quote Slide
 Dr. Dayton talks about different aspects of rebuilding societies that have undergone violence. We're going to focus on peacebuilding. One theorist I really like is John Paul Lederach. Here's his definition of conflict transformation:

"Conflict transformation is to envision and respond to the ebb and flow of social conflict as life-giving opportunities for creating constructive change processes..."
Resolution vs Transformation — Intro
 In the graphic you're seeing, Lederach contrasts the conflict resolution perspective with the conflict transformation perspective. In conflict resolution we think about ending something not desired. In conflict transformation, we want to end something destructive but we're building something desired — because conflicts don't resolve, they transform.
Resolution vs Transformation — Full
 The focus in conflict resolution is content-centered; conflict transformation looks at relationship-centered. The purpose of conflict resolution is to get a solution; transformation promotes constructive change. The time frame for conflict resolution is short term; transformation is mid or long-term. The view of conflict itself is very different: resolution seeks to de-escalate, while transformation sees conflict as dynamic.
Transformation Comparison — Final
 So when we talk about peacemaking and conflict transformation, we're talking about a much more complex, inclusive, broad approach — capturing many of the dynamics and lenses we've been discussing throughout this course.
Conflict Transformation — People, Relationships, Structures
 We're talking about transforming people, cognitions, and the way they think. Transforming relationships between groups and individuals. Addressing structures — the root causes of violence. And culture — cultural violence that perpetuates belief systems incorporating violence.
Interaction 2
Respond — Lederach's Model
Q2: Think about the conflict you are analyzing. Use Lederach's model to situate the interventions you are thinking about. Are you working in one level or multiple levels? How will your interventions move to transformation versus resolution?

Feedback: N/A
Lederach's Levels of Peacebuilding
 The graphic you're seeing is extremely famous. John Paul Lederach's approach to building peace indicates that if you're going to engage in broader peacemaking work, you have to work at many levels: the grassroots level; mid-range leadership; and ultimately top leaders if you're looking to change systems.
PA601-R23-Week 10 – 1.4  ·  Interview: Professor Esra Cuhadar
[no voiceover — interview begins]
You'll be hearing from Professor Esra Cuhadar, a Maxwell graduate who's worked extensively in inclusive negotiations with the United Nations and the US Institute of Peace.

Interview covers: the inclusivity principle; representation vs participation; modalities of inclusion (6-7 types identified); resistance to inclusive peace processes; success stories: Northern Ireland, South Africa, Nepal, Liberia.
Interaction 3
Respond — Inclusive Negotiations
Q3: Describe a negotiated agreement where more inclusiveness could have led to better societal transformation, or a negotiated settlement that was successful in both inclusiveness and societal transformation.

Feedback: N/A
PA601-R23-Week 10 – 1.5  ·  Synthesis
Where does peace work fit in our Venn diagram of the drivers of conflict? Peacemaking fits in all of the different circles — striving towards changing the structural environment; dealing with the emotional dynamics of traumatized people; shifting cognitions; and working with incompatible positions and interests toward common ground.
So peacemaking is a broad field that brings many conflict interventions into play. The key is to be facile — as John Paul Lederach says, respect the ebb and flow of the conflict. Any opportunity to take a piece of a conflict and make it more constructive is a success.
